metadata.summary: Chunking splits documents for retrieval, improving precision by 30-40%. Short=no chunking, code=code-aware, structured=structure-based, legal=semantic, default=recursive + 15% overlap.

content.title: Chunking Strategies for RAG
content.summary: Chunking splits documents into semantically meaningful pieces for embedding and retrieval. The right strategy improves retrieval precision by 30-40%. Decision rule: if short (<1000 tokens) → no chunking; if code → code-aware (AST); if structured (MD/HTML) → structure-based; if context-critical (legal/medical) → semantic; default → recursive with 15% overlap.

why.p: Embedding models have token limits; dense retrieval works better with focused content; and context windows waste tokens on irrelevant text. Over-compressed embeddings lose meaning. Chunk size and strategy directly determine whether the retriever can find the relevant passage — wrong chunking is the most common cause of RAG hallucinations from incomplete context.

list.item: Building or improving a RAG ingestion pipeline where retrieval recall or precision is suboptimal
Corpus contains mixed document types (code, markdown, PDFs, legal text) that need different splitting logic
Current chunking causes hallucinations because LLM receives incomplete context at chunk boundaries
Scaling to a large corpus where embedding cost per chunk matters
Migrating from document-level embedding to chunk-level retrieval
Documents are short (<500 tokens each) — embed at document level, no chunking needed
Prototype with <100 documents — use RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter defaults; optimize later
Cost is the primary constraint and documents are uniform — fixed-size is fastest and cheapest
Late chunking (Jina embeddings) is available and documents fit in the model's context window — late chunking outperforms all others for contextual coherence

section.name: strategy-types
section.title: Chunking Strategy Types
section.p: Eight chunking strategies for RAG ingestion, with selection criteria, parameters, and a decision framework.

rule.statement: Apply this decision framework: document < 1000 tokens → no chunking; is code → code-aware; is structured (MD/HTML) → structure-based; context-critical (legal/medical) → semantic or agentic; variable content → agentic; default → recursive with 15% overlap.
rule.rationale: Each strategy has a distinct cost/quality/speed profile. Using the wrong one (e.g., fixed-size on legal text) causes mid-sentence splits that break retrieval for entire clauses.

rule.statement: Fixed-size chunking: 512 tokens, 15% overlap. Use only for uniform plain-text where speed is critical or as a baseline.
rule.rationale: Fast and predictable but ignores semantic boundaries. A sentence split across two chunks means neither retrieves well for that concept.

rule.statement: Recursive chunking: split by paragraph → line → sentence → word (in order). This is the default for general documents. Use 512-token chunks with 77-token overlap (15%).
rule.rationale: Respects natural text boundaries without requiring an embedding API. The best default for articles, blog posts, and mixed documentation.

rule.statement: Semantic chunking: embed each sentence, split where cosine similarity drops below 0.75-0.85. Use for multi-topic documents, legal, medical, or technical content where topical coherence is critical.
rule.rationale: Preserves topic coherence within chunks, improving retrieval precision. Costs one embedding API call per sentence — only justified when quality matters more than ingestion cost.

rule.statement: Code-aware chunking: split at function/class boundaries using AST or treesitter. Never split code at fixed token counts.
rule.rationale: A function split across two chunks is nearly impossible to retrieve correctly. Code retrieval depends on the function being a complete unit.

rule.statement: Late chunking (Jina jina-embeddings-v3): embed the full document first, then pool per-span. Use when document fits in model context and pronoun/reference resolution matters.
rule.rationale: Chunk embeddings carry document-level context ("the city" knows it means Berlin). Outperforms all rule-based strategies for contextual coherence but requires a specific long-context model.

rule.statement: Agentic chunking: LLM decides strategy per document section and enriches chunks with metadata (titles, summaries). Use only when quality >> cost — one LLM call per document section.
rule.rationale: Highest quality, adaptive, adds rich metadata. Costs 10-50x more than rule-based approaches. Reserve for legal contracts, medical reports, financial filings.

section.name: rules-metrics-failure-modes
section.title: Chunking Rules, Metrics, and Failure Modes
section.p: Concrete rules for chunk size, overlap, metadata, evaluation, and the most common failure modes in RAG ingestion pipelines.

rule.statement: Default chunk size: 512 tokens. Rule of thumb: chunk_size = model_context / (top_k * 2). For 32K context with top_k=5: 512 tokens/chunk.
rule.rationale: Chunk size must leave room for the question and generated answer in the context window after top_k chunks are concatenated.

rule.statement: Default overlap: 15% (77 tokens for 512-token chunks). Increase to 25% only for dense technical content. Set to 0 for naturally complete units (functions, sections).
rule.rationale: 15% overlap is empirically optimal for most corpora. Overlap >25% causes duplicate content in top-k results, cluttering the LLM context with the same text multiple times.

rule.statement: Attach metadata to every chunk: source filename, page number, section header, chunk index, total_chunks, token_count. Never omit source path.
rule.rationale: Without source metadata, citations in RAG responses are wrong. Metadata enables filtering (e.g., retrieve only from a specific document) and is essential for debugging retrieval failures.

rule.statement: Evaluate chunking on a held-out query set (50+ representative queries) before committing to a strategy. Target: Precision@5 > 0.7, Recall@K > 0.8 for critical applications.
rule.rationale: Chunking assumptions about your corpus are almost always wrong without measurement. Defaults that work for articles may fail for PDFs or code.

list.item: Precision@K: relevant chunks in top-K / K — target > 0.7
MRR: average of 1/rank of first relevant result — target > 0.6
NDCG@10: ranking quality with relevance scores — target > 0.7
Precision@5 < 0.5: strategy too coarse; many empty chunks: parsing issue

antipattern.description: Metadata loss during chunking: chunker drops source file path or page number.
antipattern.reason: Citations in the final RAG response will be wrong. Always verify metadata propagation through the full ingestion pipeline before indexing.

antipattern.description: Overlap causes duplicate retrieval: top-k=20 returns 5 overlapping windows of the same paragraph.
antipattern.reason: LLM receives 5x the same content; responses are padded with repetition rather than diverse evidence. Deduplicate by source range before passing to reranker.

antipattern.description: Semantic chunking embedding model mismatch: using text-embedding-3-small for chunking but text-embedding-3-large for retrieval.
antipattern.reason: Similarity thresholds calibrated in one vector space are meaningless in another. Use the same model for both ingestion and retrieval.

antipattern.description: Rebuilding without re-chunking: changing chunk strategy but leaving old chunks in the same index.
antipattern.reason: Old and new chunks in the same index cause inconsistent retrieval behavior. Always rebuild the full index after changing chunking strategy.

section.name: integration-guidance
section.p: When to use chunking strategies in RAG ingestion pipelines and agentic workflows.

rule.statement: An ingestion agent receives a batch of raw documents, classifies each by type (code, markdown, legal, mixed PDF), selects the appropriate chunker, processes the batch, attaches metadata, and upserts to the vector store. For high-value documents (legal contracts, medical reports), the agent can invoke an LLM to add per-chunk summaries and section labels before embedding, improving retrieval quality at the cost of ingestion latency.
rule.rationale: Chunking decisions are logged per document type to inform future strategy tuning. Automating the choice prevents static overhead of manual configuration. LLM enrichment provides rich metadata that improves retrieval quality significantly.

section.note: Recommended subagents: Document classifier subagent that receives file extension + first 200 chars and outputs `{type: "code"|"markdown"|"legal"|"pdf"|"general", recommended_strategy: "..."}`; Chunk quality evaluator subagent that samples N chunks, computes average token count and coherence, and flags chunks that are too short (< 50 tokens) or too long (> 1500 tokens) for review.

template.purpose: YAML config template with all strategy parameters
